The cleanest way to model this is to note that every deal ("board") consists of exactly **13 tricks**, and every board played at a table involves 4 players. So:

**Tricks = (board-plays) × 13**, where a "board-play" is one deal played at one table.

**Key simplification:** In a standard pairs session, each player plays ~24 boards, and each table (4 players) plays those 24 boards. So per session:
- board-plays = (players ÷ 4) × 24
- tricks = board-plays × 13 = **78 × (number of players in that session)**

This means:

$$\text{Total tricks} \approx 78 \times (\text{total player-sessions per year})$$

### Estimating total player-sessions in Poland (2025)

- **Active tournament players:** Poland is a strong bridge nation (PZBS). Roughly ~5,000 regular + several thousand occasional players.
- **Clubs & frequency:** ~120–150 active clubs, each running 1–2 sessions/week over ~45 weeks, averaging ~30 players/session. Plus regional and national tournaments/festivals (some drawing hundreds).

Estimate:
$$140 \text{ clubs} \times 1.5 \text{ sessions/wk} \times 45 \text{ wks} \times 30 \text{ players} \approx 283{,}000$$
Adding major festivals, congresses, and national events pushes this to roughly **~320,000 player-sessions**.

### Final calculation

$$78 \times 320{,}000 \approx 25{,}000{,}000$$

### Answer

**≈ 25,000,000 tricks**
